text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Team GBhave leapt into third in theParis 2024medal table after securing gold in the equestrian team jumping final.

Britain won their first medal of day seven as Tom George and Ollie Wynne-Griffiths earned silver in the men’s pair rowing, with Jack Laugher and Anthony Harding picking up bronze in the men’s 3m synchro final before Emily Craig and Imogen Grant dominated the lightweight double sculls to earn a runaway gold three years on from heartbreak in Tokyo.

Bryony Page added a stunning gold in the women’s trampoline final, producing the routine of her life to take advantage of errors elsewhere in an emotional event, before Scott Brash, Ben Maher and Harry Charles rode to victory in the team showjumping final to grab Britain’s third gold and fifth medal of the day.

The day is also packed with athletics action, with Josh Kerr reaching the men’s 1500m in a season-best time and Dina Asher-Smith and Daryll Neita making it through to the women’s 100m semi-finals alongside Sha’Carri Richardson and Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce.

This live blog is now closed.

Time for us to leave it there for the night.

I’ll leave you withSportsbeat’s report from Paris as Team GB end the day fourth in the medal table.

We’ll be back early tomorrow morning. See you then.

Josh Kerr was as cool as can be as he stormed through to the Olympic 1500m semi-final and brushed off rival Jakob Ingebrigtsen’s latest jibe.

The 26-year-old finished fastest from the first of three heats in 3:35.83 to book his place in Sunday evening’s semis on an afternoon that also saw all three of Great Britain’s women, including European champion Dina Asher-Smith, qualify from their 100m heats on the first day of track and field action at Stade de France.

Kerr, who beat the Norwegian to the world title in 2023, was aware of – but utterly unfazed by – comments made by Ingebrigtsen, the reigning Olympic champion, who speaking to his country’s media on Thursday said of the Scotsman: “It is difficult to refer to him as a rival when he is never there. He is known as the Brit who never competes.”

But Kerr, a bear who refused to be poked, said: “I’ve heard. I would like to be defined in my career at the end of it, (and) I think I’m just getting started.

“Hopefully that’s medals, fast times and fun battles head-to-heads. Everyone can have their own opinions, I have absolutely no problems with that, and I will hopefully be able to be remembered for something slightly different than that.”

Novak Djokovic will meet Carlos Alcaraz in a blockbuster gold medal match after finally breaking his Olympic semi-final hoodoo.

Remarkably, this was the fourth time Djokovic had made it this far at the Games and the first time he has come out on top, with a bronze in 2008 his only previous medal.

Now he is guaranteed to better it after defeating Italy’s Lorenzo Musetti 6-4 6-2, setting up a rematch of the Wimbledon final, where he was well beaten by Alcaraz.

Great Britain’s men’s hockey team fell to a narrow 2-1 defeat to Germany in their latest Group A clash at Yves-du-Manoir Stadium.

Christopher Ruehr scored twice in the second quarter to strike a blow to the British team’s hopes of finishing top of the group, with both sides having already secured their place in the quarter-finals.

Chasing their first men’s hockey gold since 1988, Great Britain started well and Will Calnan thought he had opened the scoring in the first quarter, but his effort was ruled out following a video consultation.

Ruhr’s quickfire double – the second from a penalty flick – put Germany firmly in control until an impressive 49th minute effort from Gareth Furlong gave Britain hope.

STADE DE FRANCE– Joshua Cheptegei wins it for Uganda. The 2021 5,000m champion avenges defeat in this race three years ago, and in an Olympic record time.

The Ethiopians did not want to let him past but he took the lead with 500m to go and never let it go.

54.97 for the final lap. What an effort.

Beth Shriever lost her women’s Olympic BMX racing crown as she finished eighth and last in Friday’s final after her teammate Kye Whyte crashed out of the men’s competition in the semi-finals.

Shriever, twice crowned world champion since she announced her talent to the world with Olympic gold in Tokyo three years ago, had bossed every round in Thursday’s qualifying and Friday’s semi-finals only for it all to go wrong in the one that mattered most.

She was unable to recover from a poor start and Australian Saya Sakakibara, the only rider quicker than Shriever in qualifying, stormed through to take the title instead.

Defending champion Beth Shriever has breezed into the women’s BMX racing final.

Shriever looks in imperious form after winning each of her three semi-final runs.

The final takes place at 8.50pm BST.

The men’s 10,000m final is about to start, but world No 1 Daniel Ebenyo won’t be taking part. Instead, he’ll be watching the action from his sofa after not being selected by Kenya in a whirlwind of visa delays, falls and broken promises. It is a debate that has divided a nation where distance running is a huge draw.

Read more:The world No 1 missing the Olympics after Kenya’s selection gaffe

Kye Whyte has been taken to hospital after crashing out of the men’s BMX racing semi-finals.

A statement from British Cycling said: “Following his crash in tonight’s BMX racing semi-final, Kye Whyte was assessed immediately by the on-site medical team.

“This was followed up by further immediate review by Great Britain Cycling Team doctor Nigel Jones.

“Initial reports are positive but he is being taken to hospital for further assessment.

“We wish Kye a speedy recovery and look forward to seeing him on his bike very soon.”

Kye Whyte crashed out in the men’s BMX racing semi-finals at Paris 2024 and left the track on a stretcher.

The Tokyo silver medallist, who revealed after Thursday’s heats that he was struggling with a back injury, hit the dirt around 75 metres into his second run and stayed down before receiving treatment.

Whyte was moving as he was carried away on a stretcher and could be seen removing his left glove.

STADE DE FRANCE– Unbelievable scenes at the athletics.

The stadium organisers are desperately trying to quiet down the crowd – who are going wild watching Leon Marchand win gold at the swimming! (Presumably on their phones.)

I’ve never seen anything like that.

A second silver medal of the night for GB swimmers, withDuncan Scottgrabbing second place in the men’s 200m individual medley!

He finishes behind France’s Leon Marchand, who has just clocked another Olympic record to win his fourth gold medal at these Games. He’s only competed in four races!

Scott was just 0.03s shy of the British record (1:55.28) he set when winning the silver medal in Tokyo, and tonight’s podium finish – following hisgold in the 4x200m freestyle relayearlier this week – takes the Scot to eight total Olympic medals, pulling him clear of atie with Sir Chris Hoyand putting him level with Sir Bradley Wiggins on the all-time list of Team GB medal-winners.

Fellow Brit Tom Dean takes fifth in 1:56.46, just under half a second away from the podium.

Duncan Scott and Tom Dean are racing now in the final of the men’s 200m individual medley.

Scott won silver in this event in Tokyo – can either of the Britons join Ben Proud in reaching the podium tonight?

They have French superstar Leon Marchand to contend with for the gold.

GB’s Honey Osrin and Katie Shanahan, meanwhile, finished seventh and fifth respectively in the women’s 200m backstroke final.

STADE DE FRANCE– Meanwhile, the men’s shot put qualification has been progressing apace.

Quite cool innovation that both qualification groups are going at the same time, side by side.

Six-time British champion Scott Lincoln has just hurled his rock for the last time: 19.69m is only good enough for 21st and he will not be coming back for the final. Czech Republic’s Tomas Stanek leads the field on 21.61m.

Silver medal in the 50m freestyle for GB’s Ben Proud!

He’s medalled in, and won, this event at every other major international meet, and the 29-year-old Brit finally has his Olympic silverware. What a moment.

Five one-hundredths of a second separated Proud from Australia’s Cam McEvoy, who took gold in 21.25 seconds.

Only eight one-hundredths of a second then separated France’s Florent Manadou in third (21.56) from the Cayman Islands’ Jordan Crooks in eighth (21.64).

Fine margins!

STADE DE FRANCE– An AI version of Keely Hodgkinson could not have controlled that better. Towed them across the line in first to head straight into Sunday’s semi-finals. Time to visit the Louis Vuitton store tomorrow for a new bag!

STADE DE FRANCE– Big cheer for Keely Hodgkinson, must have brought the family with her.

STADE DE FRANCE– Tell you what, that was a mature run from the 17-year-old Gill.

Only the first three qualify for the semis and when Renelle Lamote overtook her on the back straight, she did not panic. She trusted the race would come back to her and indeed it did. She jogs home in third place to reach the semis.

Gill is also 13th on the UK all-time Under-15 800m list one place ahead of… Emma Raducanu!

STADE DE FRANCE– There are three British women in the 800m heats, and in fact Jemma Reekie went so quickly that she beat me back to my seat from the interview zone.

The Scot won her heat with ease in exactly two minutes, nicely in control.

Double European champion and twice Olympic silver medallist Keely Hodgkinson will go in heat four, but GB’s Phoebe Gill comes first. She’s only 17, but several people have whispered her name to me as someone who could be a big deal…

Here is a list of the key events which are taking place this evening:

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: Team GB swimmers win silverware as Hodgkinson safely through to 800m semi-final
author: George Simms, Alex Dakers, Tom Ward
description: Follow's i's live blog from day seven of the Olympic Games in Paris
